```{python}
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec
from matplotlib.patches import FancyArrowPatch, Circle, FancyBboxPatch
from matplotlib.patches import FancyBboxPatch
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
import math
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import torch
from torch import nn
from torch.utils.data import TensorDataset, DataLoader
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import IPython.display 
from IPython.display import HTML


# ── Global aesthetic settings (university light theme) ─────────────────────
BG      = "#fafaf8"        # warm off-white
CARD    = "#f0f2f5"        # soft grey card
ACCENT1 = "#1a3a6b"        # deep navy
ACCENT2 = "#1d5fa6"        # university blue
ACCENT3 = "#b5282a"        # academic red
ACCENT4 = "#2d7a4f"        # forest green
YELLOW  = "#b8860b"        # dark gold
TEXT    = "#1a1a2e"        # near-black
MUTED   = "#5a6272"        # slate grey

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor":  BG,
    "axes.facecolor":    "#ffffff",
    "axes.edgecolor":    "#d0d5dd",
    "axes.labelcolor":   TEXT,
    "xtick.color":       MUTED,
    "ytick.color":       MUTED,
    "text.color":        TEXT,
    "grid.color":        "#d0d5dd",
    "grid.linewidth":    0.6,
    "font.family":       "sans-serif",
    "font.size":         11,
    "axes.titlesize":    13,
    "axes.titleweight":  "bold",
    "lines.linewidth":   2.2,
    "axes.spines.top":   False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
})
```

## {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:88vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:0.75rem; letter-spacing:0.35em; color:#c5d4e8; text-transform:uppercase; font-weight:600; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Fundamentals of Machine Learning — Lecture 3
:::

::: {style="font-size:2.8rem; font-weight:800; line-height:1.1; text-align:center; color:#ffffff; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Linear Regression
:::

::: {style="font-size:1.1rem; color:#c5d4e8; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
A Deep Learning Perspective
:::

::: {style="width:60px; height:3px; background:#c5d4e8; border-radius:2px; margin:0.4rem auto;"}
:::

::: {style="font-size:0.85rem; color:#c5d4e8; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Zhang et al. (2023) · *Dive into Deep Learning* · Chapter 3
:::

:::

## Road Map {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.4rem; margin-top:0.6rem;"}

::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border:1px solid #1a3a6b; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
<h3>[1]{style="color:#1a3a6b;"} The Model</h3>

- From **statistics** → to **neural networks**
- Single-layer, single-output unit
- Dot-product + bias → scalar prediction
- Connection to OLS / MLE
:::

::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border:1px solid #1d5fa6; border-left:4px solid #1d5fa6; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
<h3>[2]{style="color:#1d5fa6;"} The Loss</h3>

- Mean Squared Error (MSE)
- Why squared? — MLE under Gaussian noise
- Geometry of the loss surface
- Gradient descent on a convex bowl
:::

::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border:1px solid #2d7a4f; border-left:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
<h3>[3]{style="color:#2d7a4f;"} The Optimization</h3>

- Analytical vs. iterative solutions
- Minibatch Stochastic Gradient Descent
- Learning-rate sensitivity
:::

::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border:1px solid #b5282a; border-left:4px solid #b5282a; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
<h3>[4]{style="color:#b5282a;"} D2L Code Design</h3>

- Separation of concerns
- `DataModule`, `Module`, `Trainer`
- From Scratch → Concise
:::

:::

## Part 1: The Linear Model {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:2.2rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
The Linear Model
:::

::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:1rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
Bridging classical statistics and neural networks
:::

:::

## What is Linear Regression? {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
<h3>Classical Formulation</h3>

Given $n$ examples $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^n$ with $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, predict:

$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

**Parameters:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape |
|--------|---------|-------|
| $\mathbf{w}$ | weight vector | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| $b$ | bias (intercept) | scalar |
| $\hat{y}$ | prediction | scalar |

::: {style="margin-top:0.7rem; padding:0.7rem; background:#eef1f6; border-left:3px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:4px; font-size:0.82rem;"}
**Compact form:** $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$, where $b$ absorbs via $x_0=1$
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.8

np.random.seed(42)
n = 80
x = np.linspace(0, 4, n) + 0.15*np.random.randn(n)
y = 1.8*x + 0.5 + 0.6*np.random.randn(n)

xf = np.linspace(-.2, 4.4, 200)
yf = 1.8*xf + 0.5

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4.6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG); ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')

ax.scatter(x, y, color=ACCENT1, alpha=0.7, s=40, zorder=3, label="Data points")
ax.plot(xf, yf, color=ACCENT2, lw=2.8, label=r"$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b$", zorder=4)

for xi, yi in zip(x[15:18], y[15:18]):
    yi_hat = 1.8*xi + 0.5
    ax.plot([xi, xi], [yi, yi_hat], color=ACCENT3, lw=1.4,
            linestyle="--", alpha=0.9, zorder=5)

ax.annotate("residual $\\epsilon$", xy=(x[16], (y[16]+1.8*x[16]+0.5)/2),
            xytext=(x[16]+0.5, (y[16]+1.8*x[16]+0.5)/2),
            color=ACCENT3, fontsize=10,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=ACCENT3, lw=1.3))

ax.set_xlabel("Feature $x_1$", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Target $y$", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Linear Regression — Best-fit Line", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=10, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("fig_linreg.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```

:::

:::

## From Statistics to a Neural Network {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="45%"}

<h3>The key insight 💡</h3>

A linear regression model **is** a neural network:

- **1 layer** (no hidden layers)
- **1 linear output unit**
- No nonlinear activation function

::: {style="margin-top:0.6rem;"}
$$\hat{y} = \underbrace{\sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)}_{\text{output unit}} \quad \sigma(z) = z$$
:::

::: {style="margin-top:0.7rem; padding:0.7rem; background:#edf4ef; border-left:3px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:4px; font-size:0.82rem;"}
**Vectorized mini-batch** $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w} + b$$

Each row = one example; broadcasting handles $b$.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="55%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 4.8))
ax.set_xlim(0, 10)
ax.set_ylim(0, 8)
ax.axis("off")
ax.set_facecolor(BG)
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

def circle(ax, cx, cy, r=0.42, color=ACCENT1, lw=2, label="", fontsize=11):
    c = Circle((cx, cy), r, facecolor='#ffffff', edgecolor=color, lw=lw, zorder=4)
    ax.add_patch(c)
    if label:
        ax.text(cx, cy, label, ha="center", va="center",
                fontsize=fontsize, color=color, zorder=5, fontweight="bold")

def arrow(ax, x1, y1, x2, y2, color=MUTED, lw=1.8, label=""):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(x2, y2), xytext=(x1, y1),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color,
                                lw=lw, mutation_scale=16), zorder=3)
    if label:
        mx, my = (x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2
        ax.text(mx+0.12, my+0.12, label, color=color, fontsize=9,
                ha="center", va="bottom", zorder=6)

# Input nodes
iy = [6.5, 5.0, 3.5, 2.0]
for i, y_pos in enumerate(iy):
    label = f"$x_{i+1}$" if i < 3 else "$x_d$"
    circle(ax, 2, y_pos, color=ACCENT2, label=label)
    if i == 3:
        ax.text(2, 2.8, "⋮", color=MUTED, fontsize=16, ha="center", va="center")

# Output node
circle(ax, 7.5, 4.25, r=0.55, color=ACCENT4, label="$\\hat{y}$", fontsize=13)

# Arrows from inputs to output
weights = ["$w_1$", "$w_2$", "$w_3$", "$w_d$"]
for i, (y_pos, w) in enumerate(zip(iy, weights)):
    c = [ACCENT1, ACCENT3, YELLOW, ACCENT1][i]
    arrow(ax, 2.42, y_pos, 6.95, 4.25, color=c, lw=1.8, label=w)

# Bias
circle(ax, 5.0, 1.0, r=0.38, color=YELLOW, label="$b$", fontsize=11)
arrow(ax, 5.0, 1.38, 6.95, 3.7, color=YELLOW, lw=1.6)

# Layer labels
ax.text(2, 7.3, "Input Layer\n(features)", ha="center", color=ACCENT2,
        fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
ax.text(7.5, 7.3, "Output Layer\n(linear unit)", ha="center", color=ACCENT4,
        fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
ax.text(5.0, 0.3, "Bias", ha="center", color=YELLOW, fontsize=10)

# Network equation — light background matching the theme
ax.text(5.0, 7.65, r"$\hat{y} = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 + \cdots + w_dx_d + b$",
        ha="center", color=ACCENT1, fontsize=11,
        bbox=dict(facecolor=CARD, edgecolor=ACCENT1, boxstyle="round,pad=0.45", lw=1.5))

ax.set_title("Linear Regression as a Single-Layer Neural Network",
             color=TEXT, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("fig_network.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```
:::

:::

## Statistical Connection: MLE → MSE {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

<h3>Gaussian Noise Model</h3>

Assume observations follow:

$$y^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b + \epsilon^{(i)}, \quad \epsilon^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

$$p(y^{(i)} | \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Maximizing log-likelihood:**

$$\log \mathcal{L} = -\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

::: {style="padding:0.65rem; background:#edf4ef; border:1px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:6px; font-size:0.83rem; margin-top:0.6rem;"}
**→ Maximizing $\log\mathcal{L}$ ≡ Minimizing MSE**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{1}{2}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.8

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4.6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

# Left: Gaussian likelihood
ax = axes[0]
ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
z = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)
for sigma, col, lbl in [(0.5, ACCENT1, "$\\sigma=0.5$"),
                         (1.0, ACCENT2, "$\\sigma=1.0$"),
                         (2.0, ACCENT4, "$\\sigma=2.0$")]:
    pdf = np.exp(-z**2/(2*sigma**2)) / (np.sqrt(2*np.pi)*sigma)
    ax.plot(z, pdf, color=col, lw=2.3, label=lbl)
ax.fill_between(z, 0,
    np.exp(-z**2/(2*1.0**2)) / (np.sqrt(2*np.pi)*1.0),
    alpha=0.12, color=ACCENT2)
ax.set_xlabel("Residual $\\epsilon$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("$p(\\epsilon)$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Gaussian Noise Model", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Right: MSE loss surface — navy/blue palette matching theme
ax2 = axes[1]
ax2.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
w = np.linspace(-2, 4, 250)
b_arr = np.linspace(-2, 4, 250)
W, B = np.meshgrid(w, b_arr)
L = (W - 1.8)**2 + (B - 0.5)**2 + 0.5*(W-1.8)*(B-0.5)
contours = ax2.contourf(W, B, L, levels=25, cmap="Blues", alpha=0.88)
ax2.contour(W, B, L, levels=10, colors=MUTED, alpha=0.25, linewidths=0.7)
ax2.plot(1.8, 0.5, "*", color=ACCENT3, markersize=16, zorder=5,
         label="Optimum $(w^*=1.8,\\ b^*=0.5)$")

ww, bb = -1.5, 3.5
path_w, path_b = [ww], [bb]
for _ in range(40):
    gw = 2*(ww-1.8) + 0.5*(bb-0.5)
    gb = 2*(bb-0.5) + 0.5*(ww-1.8)
    ww -= 0.12*gw; bb -= 0.12*gb
    path_w.append(ww); path_b.append(bb)
ax2.plot(path_w, path_b, "o-", color=ACCENT4, ms=3, lw=1.6, alpha=0.9,
         label="GD path")
ax2.plot(path_w[0], path_b[0], "s", color=YELLOW, ms=8, zorder=6, label="Start")

ax2.set_xlabel("$w$", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel("$b$", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_title("MSE Loss Landscape", fontsize=11)
ax2.legend(fontsize=8.5, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT,
           loc='upper right')
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.2)

plt.colorbar(contours, ax=ax2, label="Loss $\\mathcal{L}$",
             fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
fig.tight_layout(pad=1.2)
plt.savefig("fig_mle_mse.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```

:::

:::

## Part 2: Loss & Optimization {background-color="#1d5fa6"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:2.2rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Training: Loss & Optimization
:::

::: {style="color:#ccddf0; font-size:1rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
From closed-form solutions to minibatch SGD
:::

:::

## The MSE Loss Function {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}

<h3>Definition</h3>

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2}_{\ell^{(i)}}$$

The $\frac{1}{2}$ factor cancels the $2$ from differentiation:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y$$

<h3>Analytical (Normal Equation) Solution</h3>

When $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank:

$$\mathbf{w}^* = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

::: {style="padding:0.65rem; background:#fdf8ed; border-left:3px solid #b8860b; border-radius:4px; font-size:0.82rem; margin-top:0.6rem;"}
**⚡ In practice**: $O(d^3)$ cost — infeasible for large $d$.  
**Iterative gradient descent** scales to millions of parameters.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.8

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7, 4.6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

ax = axes[0]
ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
w_vals = np.linspace(-1, 4, 300)
loss_vals = 0.5 * (w_vals - 2)**2 * 3 + 0.4*(w_vals - 2)**4 * 0.1

ax.plot(w_vals, loss_vals, color=ACCENT1, lw=2.8)
ax.fill_between(w_vals, 0, loss_vals, alpha=0.12, color=ACCENT1)
ax.axvline(2.0, color=ACCENT4, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="$w^* = 2$")
ax.plot(2.0, 0.0, "o", color=ACCENT4, ms=9, zorder=5)

for ww in [-0.5, 0.6, 3.5, 3.0]:
    lw_val = 0.5 * (ww - 2)**2 * 3 + 0.4*(ww-2)**4*0.1
    grad = (ww-2)*3 + 0.4*(ww-2)**3
    ax.annotate("", xy=(ww - 0.35*np.sign(grad), lw_val - 0.08),
                xytext=(ww, lw_val),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT3,
                                lw=1.8, mutation_scale=13))

ax.set_xlabel("Parameter $w$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Loss $\\mathcal{L}(w)$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Convex Loss — 1D", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9.5, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax.set_ylim(bottom=-0.3)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax2 = axes[1]
ax2.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
epochs_lr = np.arange(1, 31)
for lr, col, lbl in [(0.01, ACCENT3, "lr=0.01 (slow)"),
                      (0.1,  ACCENT2, "lr=0.10 (good)"),
                      (0.45, YELLOW,  "lr=0.45 (unstable)")]:
    losses_lr = [10 * (1 - lr)**e for e in epochs_lr]
    if lr == 0.45:
        losses_lr = [10 * abs(1 - lr)**e * (1 + 0.3*np.sin(e)) for e in epochs_lr]
    ax2.plot(epochs_lr, losses_lr, color=col, lw=2.3, label=lbl)

ax2.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel("Training Loss", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_title("Learning Rate Sensitivity", fontsize=11)
ax2.legend(fontsize=9, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax2.set_ylim(bottom=0)
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

fig.tight_layout(pad=1.2)
plt.savefig("fig_loss_lr.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```

:::

:::

## Minibatch Stochastic Gradient Descent {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="45%"}

<h3>Update Rule</h3>

$$(\mathbf{w}, b) \leftarrow (\mathbf{w}, b) - \frac{\eta}{|\mathcal{B}|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla_{(\mathbf{w}, b)}\, \ell^{(i)}$$

**Gradients** for the MSE loss:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}|}\mathbf{X}_\mathcal{B}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{y}}_\mathcal{B} - \mathbf{y}_\mathcal{B})$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}|}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$$

::: {style="margin-top:0.6rem;"}

| Variant | Batch size |
|---------|-----------|
| Full-batch GD | $n$ (all data) |
| **Minibatch SGD** | **$|\mathcal{B}| \ll n$** |
| Stochastic GD | $1$ |

:::

::: {style="padding:0.55rem; background:#eef1f6; border-left:3px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:4px; font-size:0.82rem; margin-top:0.6rem;"}
Minibatch SGD balances **computational efficiency** (GPU parallelism) with **noisy but fast convergence**.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="55%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5

np.random.seed(0)

n_samples = 200
true_w = np.array([2.0, -1.5])
true_b = 0.5
X_data = np.random.randn(n_samples, 2)
y_data = X_data @ true_w + true_b + 0.3*np.random.randn(n_samples)

def train_sgd(X, y, lr, batch_size, epochs=30):
    w = np.zeros(2); b = 0.0
    losses = []
    n = len(y)
    for _ in range(epochs):
        idx = np.random.permutation(n)
        for start in range(0, n, batch_size):
            batch = idx[start:start+batch_size]
            Xb, yb = X[batch], y[batch]
            y_hat = Xb @ w + b
            err = y_hat - yb
            w -= lr / len(batch) * Xb.T @ err
            b -= lr / len(batch) * err.sum()
        y_hat_all = X @ w + b
        losses.append(np.mean(0.5*(y_hat_all - y)**2))
    return losses

epochs_sgd = 30
configs = [
    (0.1, 1,   ACCENT3, "SGD (|B|=1)"),
    (0.1, 16,  ACCENT2, "Minibatch (|B|=16)"),
    (0.1, 64,  ACCENT4, "Minibatch (|B|=64)"),
    (0.1, 200, YELLOW,  "Full-batch GD"),
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.5, 4.8))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

ax = axes[0]
ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
for lr, bs, col, lbl in configs:
    losses = train_sgd(X_data, y_data, lr, bs, epochs_sgd)
    ax.plot(range(1, epochs_sgd+1), losses, color=col, lw=2.1, label=lbl, alpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("MSE Loss", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Convergence by Batch Size", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax2 = axes[1]
ax2.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
w_hist = [[0.0, 0.0]]; b_hist = [0.0]
w = np.array([0.0, 0.0]); b = 0.0
lr = 0.1; bs = 16
for _ in range(epochs_sgd):
    idx = np.random.permutation(n_samples)
    for start in range(0, n_samples, bs):
        batch = idx[start:start+bs]
        Xb, yb = X_data[batch], y_data[batch]
        y_hat = Xb @ w + b
        err = y_hat - yb
        w -= lr / len(batch) * Xb.T @ err
        b -= lr / len(batch) * err.sum()
    w_hist.append(w.copy()); b_hist.append(b)

ep = np.arange(epochs_sgd+1)
ax2.plot(ep, [wh[0] for wh in w_hist], color=ACCENT1, lw=2.3,
         label=f"$w_1$ (true={true_w[0]})")
ax2.plot(ep, [wh[1] for wh in w_hist], color=ACCENT3, lw=2.3,
         label=f"$w_2$ (true={true_w[1]})")
ax2.plot(ep, b_hist, color=ACCENT4, lw=2.3,
         label=f"$b$ (true={true_b})")

ax2.axhline(true_w[0], color=ACCENT1, ls="--", lw=1.1, alpha=0.6)
ax2.axhline(true_w[1], color=ACCENT3, ls="--", lw=1.1, alpha=0.6)
ax2.axhline(true_b,   color=ACCENT4, ls="--", lw=1.1, alpha=0.6)

ax2.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel("Parameter value", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_title("Parameter Convergence (|B|=16)", fontsize=11)
ax2.legend(fontsize=9, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

fig.tight_layout(pad=1.2)
plt.savefig("fig_sgd.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```

:::

:::


## Part 3: Implementation from Scratch {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

:::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}
::: {style="font-size:2.8rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif; text-shadow: 2px 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);"}
Linear Regression: From Scratch 🛠️
:::
::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:1.3rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
Build the data pipeline, model, loss, optimizer, and training loop by hand.
:::
::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:0.85rem; text-align:center;"}
D2L §3.2: tensors + automatic differentiation, before using high-level layers.
:::
::::

## D2L §3.2: What We Build Ourselves {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1.05fr 0.95fr; gap:1.2rem; align-items:center;"}

::: 
To understand what frameworks automate, D2L first implements every moving part directly.

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:0.9rem; margin-top:1rem;"}
::: {style="background:#eef1f6; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px;"}
**1. Data**

Generate synthetic examples and yield shuffled minibatches.
:::

::: {style="background:#edf4ef; border-left:4px solid #2d7a4f; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px;"}
**2. Parameters**

Create $w$ and $b$, then ask Autograd to track gradients.
:::

::: {style="background:#fdf8ed; border-left:4px solid #b8860b; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px;"}
**3. Model + loss**

$\hat{y}=Xw+b$, then half squared loss $\frac{1}{2}(\hat{y}-y)^2$.
:::

::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; border-left:4px solid #b5282a; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px;"}
**4. Optimizer**

Use minibatch SGD: compute gradients, update, clear gradients.
:::
:::
:::

::: 
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5.4
#| fig-width: 6.0

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5.4))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#fafaf8')
ax.axis('off')

items = [
    (0.3, 3.9, "Synthetic data\nX, y", "#1a3a6b", "#eef1f6"),
    (0.3, 1.7, "Parameters\nw, b", "#2d7a4f", "#edf4ef"),
    (3.0, 3.0, "Model\nŷ = Xw + b", "#4a4a4a", "#f0f2f5"),
    (5.7, 3.0, "Loss\n½(ŷ - y)²", "#b8860b", "#fdf8ed"),
    (3.0, 0.8, "SGD\nupdate + zero", "#b5282a", "#f9eaeb"),
]

for x, y, label, edge, face in items:
    patch = FancyBboxPatch((x, y), 1.9, 0.95, boxstyle="round,pad=0.12",
                           ec=edge, fc=face, lw=2)
    ax.add_patch(patch)
    ax.text(x + 0.95, y + 0.48, label, ha="center", va="center",
            color=edge, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")

arrow = dict(arrowstyle="->", lw=2)
ax.annotate("", xy=(3.0, 3.45), xytext=(2.2, 4.2), arrowprops={**arrow, "color":"#1a3a6b"})
ax.annotate("", xy=(3.0, 3.0), xytext=(2.2, 2.25), arrowprops={**arrow, "color":"#2d7a4f"})
ax.annotate("", xy=(5.7, 3.45), xytext=(4.9, 3.45), arrowprops={**arrow, "color":"#4a4a4a"})
ax.annotate("", xy=(3.95, 1.75), xytext=(6.6, 2.95), arrowprops={**arrow, "color":"#b8860b"})
ax.annotate("", xy=(1.25, 1.7), xytext=(3.0, 1.25), arrowprops={**arrow, "color":"#b5282a"})

ax.text(5.7, 1.85, "backward()", color="#b8860b", fontsize=10, fontstyle="italic")
ax.text(1.95, 0.72, "new w, b", color="#b5282a", fontsize=10, fontstyle="italic")
ax.set_xlim(0, 8)
ax.set_ylim(0.3, 5.1)
plt.show()
```
:::

:::

## 1️⃣ Generating Synthetic Data {background-color="#fafaf8"}

We create data from the same model used in D2L:
$$y = Xw + b + \epsilon,\quad w=[2,-3.4]^\top,\quad b=4.2,\quad \epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,0.01^2).$$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{python}
#| echo: true
import torch
import random

torch.manual_seed(42)
random.seed(42)

def synthetic_data(w, b, num_examples):
    """Generate y = Xw + b + noise."""
    X = torch.normal(0, 1, (num_examples, len(w)))
    y = torch.matmul(X, w) + b
    y += torch.normal(0, 0.01, y.shape)
    return X, y.reshape((-1, 1))

true_w = torch.tensor([2, -3.4])
true_b = 4.2
features, labels = synthetic_data(true_w, true_b, 1000)
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
```{python}
#| echo: true
print(f'features[0]: {features[0]}')
print(f'label[0]:    {labels[0]}')
```
:::

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5.2
#| fig-width: 5.2
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio

pio.renderers.default = "plotly_mimetype+notebook_connected"

X_np = features[:300].numpy()
y_np = labels[:300].numpy().flatten()

fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Scatter3d(
    x=X_np[:, 0],
    y=X_np[:, 1],
    z=y_np,
    mode='markers',
    marker=dict(
        color=y_np,
        colorscale='RdBu',
        reversescale=True,
        opacity=0.8,
        size=4
    )
)])

fig.update_layout(
    paper_bgcolor='#fafaf8',
    scene=dict(
        bgcolor='#fafaf8',
        xaxis_title='Feature x1',
        yaxis_title='Feature x2',
        yaxis=dict(range=[-3, 3]),
        zaxis_title='Target y',
        camera=dict(
            eye=dict(x=1.6, y=-1.6, z=0.9),
            center=dict(x=0, y=0, z=0),
            up=dict(x=0, y=0, z=1)
        ),        
        aspectmode='manual',
        aspectratio=dict(x=1, y=1, z=0.8)
    ),
    title=dict(
        text='Synthetic Linear Data',
        font=dict(color='#1a3a6b')
    ),
    margin=dict(l=0, r=0, b=0, t=40)
)

fig.show()
```
:::
:::

## 2️⃣ Reading Data in Minibatches {background-color="#fafaf8"}

D2L shuffles examples and yields minibatches: each update uses a small random sample instead of the whole dataset.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

```{python}
#| echo: true
def data_iter(batch_size, features, labels):
    """Yield shuffled minibatches of data."""
    num_examples = len(features)
    indices = list(range(num_examples))
    random.shuffle(indices)

    for i in range(0, num_examples, batch_size):
        batch_indices = torch.tensor(
            indices[i: min(i + batch_size, num_examples)]
        )
        yield features[batch_indices], labels[batch_indices]

batch_size = 10
for X_batch, y_batch in data_iter(batch_size, features, labels):
    print(f'X batch shape: {X_batch.shape}')
    print(f'y batch shape: {y_batch.shape}')
    break
```

:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {style="background:#eef1f6; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; margin-bottom:1rem;"}
**Why shuffle?**  
Random order reduces systematic order effects and gives minibatch gradients that better represent the dataset.
:::

::: {style="background:#fdf8ed; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #b8860b;"}
**Why minibatches?**  
They balance two extremes: full-batch updates are expensive; one-example updates are noisy. Minibatches also let hardware process examples in parallel.
:::
:::
:::

## A Useful Warning from D2L {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.2rem; align-items:start;"}

::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; padding:1.2rem; border-radius:8px; border-top:4px solid #1a3a6b;"}
**Our handwritten iterator is didactic**

- Easy to inspect
- Shows what “minibatch” means
- Good for understanding the training loop
:::

::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; padding:1.2rem; border-radius:8px; border-top:4px solid #b5282a;"}
**But it is not production-grade**

- Assumes all data fits in memory
- Does many random memory accesses
- Cannot naturally stream large files
:::

:::

::: {style="margin-top:1.1rem; background:#edf4ef; border-left:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1rem;"}
This is exactly why D2L moves from scratch code in §3.2 to framework data loaders in §3.3.
:::

## 3️⃣ Initializing Model Parameters {background-color="#fafaf8"}

Before SGD can update anything, we need parameters and gradients.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Small random weights, as in D2L
w = torch.normal(0, 0.01, size=(2, 1),
                 requires_grad=True)

# Bias starts at zero
b = torch.zeros(1, requires_grad=True)

print(f'w: {w.data.T}')
print(f'b: {b.data}')
```

::: {style="background:#eef1f6; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; margin-top:1rem;"}
`requires_grad=True` tells PyTorch to record operations involving this tensor so `backward()` can compute derivatives.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 0.9em; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);">
  <tr style="background:#1a3a6b; color:white;">
    <th style="padding:10px; border-radius:8px 0 0 0;">Parameter</th>
    <th style="padding:10px;">Shape</th>
    <th style="padding:10px; border-radius:0 8px 0 0;">Init</th>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#f0f2f5;">
    <td style="padding:10px; font-weight:bold;">Weights ($w$)</td>
    <td style="padding:10px;"><code>(2, 1)</code></td>
    <td style="padding:10px;">$\mathcal{N}(0, 0.01)$</td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#ffffff;">
    <td style="padding:10px; font-weight:bold; border-radius:0 0 0 8px;">Bias ($b$)</td>
    <td style="padding:10px;"><code>(1,)</code></td>
    <td style="padding:10px; border-radius:0 0 8px 0;">0</td>
  </tr>
</table>

::: {style="margin-top:1rem; padding:0.9rem; border-radius:8px; background:#fdf8ed; border-left:4px solid #b8860b;"}
For this **single-output linear model**, zero weights would still learn. Symmetry breaking becomes critical when multiple neurons in the same layer could otherwise remain identical.
:::
:::
:::

## 4️⃣ Model and Loss Function {background-color="#fafaf8"}

Now we write the two equations at the center of D2L §3.2.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Model
$$\hat{y} = Xw + b$$

```{python}
#| echo: true
def linreg(X, w, b):
    """The linear regression model."""
    return torch.matmul(X, w) + b
```

::: {style="text-align:center; padding:1rem; color:#1a3a6b; font-weight:700;"}
Predictions flow into the loss.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### The Loss
$$\ell(\hat{y}, y) = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$$

```{python}
#| echo: true
def squared_loss(y_hat, y):
    """Half squared loss, matching D2L."""
    y = y.reshape(y_hat.shape)
    return (y_hat - y) ** 2 / 2
```

::: {style="background:#fdf8ed; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #b8860b; margin-top:1rem;"}
The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ does not change the optimum. It makes the derivative cleaner: $\partial \ell / \partial \hat{y} = \hat{y}-y$.
:::
:::
:::

## 5️⃣ The Optimizer: Minibatch SGD {background-color="#fafaf8"}

For each minibatch $B$, D2L updates parameters using the average minibatch gradient:
$$ (w,b) \leftarrow (w,b) - \eta \frac{1}{|B|}\sum_{i\in B}\nabla_{w,b}\ell_i.$$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{python}
#| echo: true
def sgd(params, lr, batch_size):
    """Minibatch stochastic gradient descent."""
    with torch.no_grad():
        for param in params:
            param -= lr * param.grad / batch_size
            param.grad.zero_()
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #b5282a; margin-bottom:1rem;"}
**Why divide by `batch_size`?**  
The code calls `l.sum().backward()`, so the gradients are summed over the batch. Dividing gives the average minibatch gradient.
:::

::: {style="background:#eef1f6; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b;"}
**Why `zero_()`?**  
PyTorch accumulates gradients. Clearing them prevents batch 2 from adding onto batch 1.
:::
:::
:::

## The Training Loop {background-color="#fafaf8"}

This is the reusable loop pattern D2L wants us to recognize: forward pass, loss, backward pass, optimizer step.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="56%"}

```{python}
#| echo: true
lr = 0.03
num_epochs = 3
net = linreg
loss = squared_loss

epoch_losses = []

for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for X, y in data_iter(batch_size, features, labels):
        l = loss(net(X, w, b), y)
        l.sum().backward()
        sgd([w, b], lr, batch_size)

    with torch.no_grad():
        train_l = loss(net(features, w, b), labels)
        epoch_losses.append(train_l.mean().item())
        print(f'epoch {epoch+1}, loss {float(train_l.mean()):f}')
```

:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.8
#| fig-width: 5.1
import numpy as np

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.1, 4.8))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#fafaf8')
ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')

epochs = list(range(1, len(epoch_losses) + 1))
ax.plot(epochs, epoch_losses, marker='o', markersize=9,
        linewidth=3, color='#1a3a6b', markerfacecolor='#b5282a')

ax.set_xticks(epochs)
ax.set_title('Average Half Squared Loss', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', color='#1a3a6b')
ax.set_xlabel('Epoch', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel(r'Mean of $\frac{1}{2}(\hat{y}-y)^2$', fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.show()
```
:::
:::

## Did We Recover the True Parameters? {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
```{python}
#| echo: true
print(f'w error: {true_w - w.reshape(true_w.shape)}')
print(f'b error: {true_b - b}')
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {style="background:#edf4ef; border-left:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.2rem;"}
The estimates should be **close** to the true parameters because:

- the data was generated by a linear model,
- the noise is small,
- the loss is convex,
- SGD sees the whole dataset each epoch.
:::

::: {style="background:#fdf8ed; border-left:4px solid #b8860b; border-radius:8px; padding:1rem; margin-top:1rem;"}
D2L’s caution: in machine learning, prediction accuracy usually matters more than exactly recovering hidden ground-truth parameters.
:::
:::
:::

## Part 4: Concise Implementation {background-color="#b5282a"}

:::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}
::: {style="font-size:2.8rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif; text-shadow: 2px 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);"}
Concise Implementation ⚡
:::
::: {style="color:#f9eaeb; font-size:1.3rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
D2L §3.3: keep the same training idea, replace repeated machinery with framework components.
:::
::::

## From Scratch → Framework Components {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
<table style="width:100%; border-collapse: collapse; font-size: 1.02em; text-align:left; box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1); margin-top:1rem;">
  <tr style="background:#b5282a; color:white; border-bottom:3px solid #7a1b1c;">
    <th style="padding:14px; border-radius:8px 0 0 0;">Concern</th>
    <th style="padding:14px;">From Scratch</th>
    <th style="padding:14px; border-radius:0 8px 0 0;">PyTorch API</th>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#ffffff; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd;">
    <td style="padding:14px; font-weight:bold; color:#1a3a6b;">Data</td>
    <td style="padding:14px;">Manual shuffling and `yield`</td>
    <td style="padding:14px; background:#f9eaeb;"><code>TensorDataset</code> + <code>DataLoader</code></td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#f0f2f5; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd;">
    <td style="padding:14px; font-weight:bold; color:#1a3a6b;">Model</td>
    <td style="padding:14px;">Manual tensors + `matmul`</td>
    <td style="padding:14px; background:#f9eaeb;"><code>nn.Linear(2, 1)</code></td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#ffffff; border-bottom:1px solid #ddd;">
    <td style="padding:14px; font-weight:bold; color:#1a3a6b;">Loss</td>
    <td style="padding:14px;">Function returning half squared loss</td>
    <td style="padding:14px; background:#f9eaeb;"><code>nn.MSELoss()</code> with built-in reduction</td>
  </tr>
  <tr style="background:#f0f2f5;">
    <td style="padding:14px; font-weight:bold; color:#1a3a6b; border-radius:0 0 0 8px;">Optimization</td>
    <td style="padding:14px;">Manual update + clear gradients</td>
    <td style="padding:14px; background:#f9eaeb; border-radius:0 0 8px 0;"><code>torch.optim.SGD</code></td>
  </tr>
</table>
:::

::: {style="margin-top:1rem; background:#eef1f6; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1rem;"}
The deep-learning pattern stays the same; the implementation becomes easier to swap, test, and reuse.
:::

## 1️⃣ Concise Data Loading {background-color="#fafaf8"}

`TensorDataset` pairs features and labels. `DataLoader` handles batching and optional shuffling each epoch.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{python}
#| echo: true
from torch.utils.data import TensorDataset, DataLoader

def load_array(data_arrays, batch_size, is_train=True):
    """Construct a PyTorch data iterator."""
    dataset = TensorDataset(*data_arrays)
    return DataLoader(dataset, batch_size, shuffle=is_train)

batch_size = 10
data_iter_concise = load_array((features, labels), batch_size)
print(next(iter(data_iter_concise))[0].shape)
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; padding:1.2rem; border-radius:8px; border-top:4px solid #b5282a;"}
**What the abstraction provides**

- standard iterator interface
- batching and shuffling
- support for larger data pipelines
- easy swap from synthetic data to real datasets
:::
:::
:::

## 2️⃣ Concise Model, Loss & Optimizer {background-color="#fafaf8"}

The framework owns the layer object, parameter collection, and optimizer bookkeeping.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}

```{python}
#| echo: true
from torch import nn

net_concise = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(2, 1))

# Match D2L's initialization: small random weights, zero bias
net_concise[0].weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01)
net_concise[0].bias.data.fill_(0)

loss_concise = nn.MSELoss()
trainer_concise = torch.optim.SGD(
    net_concise.parameters(), lr=0.03
)

for param in net_concise.parameters():
    print(param.shape)
```
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 5.1
#| fig-width: 4.6
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.6, 5.1))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#fafaf8')
ax.axis('off')

ax.scatter([1, 1], [3, 1], s=950, color='#1a3a6b', zorder=2)
ax.text(1, 3, '$x_1$', ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontweight='bold', fontsize=14)
ax.text(1, 1, '$x_2$', ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontweight='bold', fontsize=14)
ax.scatter([3], [2], s=1100, color='#b5282a', zorder=2)
ax.text(3, 2, '$\hat{y}$', ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontweight='bold', fontsize=16)
ax.plot([1, 3], [3, 2], color='#4a4a4a', lw=3, zorder=1)
ax.plot([1, 3], [1, 2], color='#4a4a4a', lw=3, zorder=1)
ax.text(2, 2.8, '$w_1$', ha='center', color='#4a4a4a', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(2, 1.2, '$w_2$', ha='center', color='#4a4a4a', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(3, 2.7, '+$b$', ha='center', color='#b8860b', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_title('nn.Linear(2, 1)', fontweight='bold', color='#1a3a6b', y=1.04)
plt.show()
```
:::
:::

## 3️⃣ Concise Training Loop {background-color="#fafaf8"}

D2L’s key message: high-level APIs shorten setup, but the loop is still familiar.

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{python}
#| echo: true
num_epochs = 3
for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for X, y in data_iter_concise:
        l = loss_concise(net_concise(X), y)
        trainer_concise.zero_grad()
        l.backward()
        trainer_concise.step()

    l = loss_concise(net_concise(features), labels)
    print(f'epoch {epoch + 1}, loss {l:f}')
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #1a3a6b; margin-bottom:0.8rem;"}
`zero_grad()` clears old gradients.
:::
::: {style="background:#fdf8ed; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #b8860b; margin-bottom:0.8rem;"}
`backward()` computes gradients using the computational graph.
:::
::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; padding:1rem; border-radius:8px; border-left:4px solid #b5282a;"}
`step()` applies the SGD update to every registered parameter.
:::
:::
:::

## The Payoff: Swappable Parts {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:1rem; margin-top:1rem;"}

::: {style="background:#eef1f6; border-top:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**Swap data**

Keep the model and optimizer, replace the dataset or `DataLoader`.
:::

::: {style="background:#edf4ef; border-top:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**Swap model**

Replace `nn.Linear` with a deeper network; keep the loop structure.
:::

::: {style="background:#f9eaeb; border-top:4px solid #b5282a; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**Swap optimizer**

Change `SGD` to `Adam` in one line; the training loop stays recognizable.
:::

:::

::: {style="margin-top:1.3rem; background:#fdf8ed; border-left:4px solid #b8860b; border-radius:8px; padding:1rem;"}
This separation of concerns is the bridge from a toy linear model to larger deep-learning systems.
:::

## Check Your Understanding {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.1rem; margin-top:1rem;"}

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**1. Zero weights**

Would zero initialization still train this single-output linear model? Why is the answer different for multiple hidden neurons?
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b5282a; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**2. The `1/2` factor**

The scratch loss uses $\frac{1}{2}(\hat{y}-y)^2$, but `nn.MSELoss()` does not. What changes: the optimum or the gradient scale?
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**3. Forgetting `zero_grad()`**

What happens to gradients on batch 2 and batch 3 if we never clear them?
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b8860b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**4. One-line swap**

Change SGD to Adam. Which line changes, and which parts of the pipeline stay untouched?
:::
:::

:::

## Part 5: Generalization & Regularization {background-color="#2d7a4f"}

:::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}
::: {style="font-size:2.8rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif; text-shadow: 2px 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);"}
Generalization & Regularization 🛡️
:::
::: {style="color:#edf4ef; font-size:1.3rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
Overfitting, Underfitting, and Weight Decay
:::
::: {style="margin-top:2rem; padding:1rem 2rem; background:rgba(255,255,255,0.1); border-radius:12px; color:white; font-weight:bold; border: 2px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.5);"}
Test the Framework, Tune or Applictionin real World
:::
::::


## Part 1: The Linear Model {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:2.2rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Generalization Error / Training Error
:::

::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:1rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
From the model Training to the real World
:::

:::


## Training Error Generalization Error

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
The Central Tradeoff when fitting the Models lies within In sample Fit, and General Applicapility on similar Data.

- The Errors we observe witin our Training Data we call: "Training Error"
- The Errors that would occure general we call : "Generaliation Error"
- The Generalization Error includes the Training Error
:::

::: {.column width="54%"}

![](Tr_Ge_Err.png){width=90%}

:::
:::

## Training Error

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width=42%}

- Average of the Training Losses:
$$
{\large
    R_{emp}[X, y, f] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \color{darkred}{l(x^{(i)},y^{(i)},f(x^{(i)}))}
}%
$$

- in our Case losses are $L(w,b)$ :
$$
{\large
    L(w,b) = \frac{1}{2n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(w^\intercal x^{(i)}+b - y^{(i)})^2
}%
$$
:::

:::{.column width=42%}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 4.8

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(5.25, 3.45))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

ax = axes[0]
ax.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
w_vals = np.linspace(-1, 4, 300)
loss_vals = 0.5 * (w_vals - 2)**2 * 3 + 0.4*(w_vals - 2)**4 * 0.1

ax.plot(w_vals, loss_vals, color=ACCENT1, lw=2.8)
ax.fill_between(w_vals, 0, loss_vals, alpha=0.12, color=ACCENT1)
ax.axvline(2.0, color=ACCENT4, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="$w^* = 2$")
ax.plot(2.0, 0.0, "o", color=ACCENT4, ms=9, zorder=5)

for ww in [-0.5, 0.6, 3.5, 3.0]:
    lw_val = 0.5 * (ww - 2)**2 * 3 + 0.4*(ww-2)**4*0.1
    grad = (ww-2)*3 + 0.4*(ww-2)**3
    ax.annotate("", xy=(ww - 0.35*np.sign(grad), lw_val - 0.08),
                xytext=(ww, lw_val),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT3,
                                lw=1.8, mutation_scale=13))

ax.set_xlabel("Parameter $w$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Loss $\\mathcal{L}(w)$", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Convex Loss — 1D", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9.5, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax.set_ylim(bottom=-0.3)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax2 = axes[1]
ax2.set_facecolor('#ffffff')
epochs_lr = np.arange(1, 31)
for lr, col, lbl in [(0.01, ACCENT3, "lr=0.01 (slow)"),
                      (0.1,  ACCENT2, "lr=0.10 (good)"),
                      (0.45, YELLOW,  "lr=0.45 (unstable)")]:
    losses_lr = [10 * (1 - lr)**e for e in epochs_lr]
    if lr == 0.45:
        losses_lr = [10 * abs(1 - lr)**e * (1 + 0.3*np.sin(e)) for e in epochs_lr]
    ax2.plot(epochs_lr, losses_lr, color=col, lw=2.3, label=lbl)

ax2.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_ylabel("Training Loss", fontsize=10)
ax2.set_title("Learning Rate Sensitivity", fontsize=11)
ax2.legend(fontsize=9, facecolor=CARD, edgecolor="#c0c5cc", labelcolor=TEXT)
ax2.set_ylim(bottom=0)
ax2.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

fig.tight_layout(pad=1.2)
plt.savefig("fig_loss_lr.png", dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight", facecolor=BG)
plt.show()
```

:::
:::

## Theory: Generalization Error

:::{layout-ncol=2}

::: {.left}

$$
{\large
    R[p,f] = E(x,y) \sim p[L(w,b)]
}%
$$

### Probability of the Data given the Truth ###
- is not observable but the Startingpoint of our Rationale

### likelihood of the Model ###   
- can be understood by the losses of our Model

### Joint: Generalization Error ###
$$
{\Large
    R[p,f] = \int \int \left[ L(w,b) \cdot p(x,y) \right] \mathrm{d}x \mathrm{d}y
}%
$$

:::

```{python}
#|echo: false

# Grid over x and y
xs = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
ys = np.linspace(-2, 2, 80)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(xs, ys)

# Simple predictor: f(x) = x
fX = X

# Pointwise loss
loss = (Y - fX) ** 2

# Simple joint density p(x,y) = p(x)p(y)
sigma_x = 1.0
sigma_y = 1.0

px = (1 / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi * sigma_x**2)) * np.exp(-(X**2) / (2 * sigma_x**2))
py = (1 / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi * sigma_y**2)) * np.exp(-(Y**2) / (2 * sigma_y**2))
p_xy = px * py

# Local contribution to expected loss
Z = loss * p_xy

fig = go.Figure(
    data=[
        go.Surface(
            x=X,
            y=Y,
            z=Z,
            colorscale='RdBu',
            reversescale=True,
            showscale=True,
            colorbar=dict(
                thickness=12,
                len=0.65
            )
        )
    ]
)   

fig.update_layout(
    paper_bgcolor="#fafaf8",    
    title=r"",
    scene=dict(
        bgcolor="#fafaf8",
        xaxis_title="x",
        yaxis_title="y",
        zaxis_title="L(w,b) · p(x)",
        camera=dict(
            eye=dict(x=1.6, y=1.6, z=0.9)
        )
    ),
    width=600,
    height=460,
    margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=0, b=0)
)

# Show in notebook
fig.show()

# Export for Reveal.js
fig.write_html(
    "expected_loss_surface.html",
    include_plotlyjs=True,   # fully self-contained
    full_html=True
)
```

:::

## Cross-Validation

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width=44%}

- The Theory requires, prior knowledge of the True Model
- If we already new the real True Model,why woul w ebuilkd a estimate?
- In Practice we use Crossvalidation to Approximate our Generalization Error

$$
{\large
    E_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i = 1}^{k} Ei 
}%
$$
:::

:::{.column width=52%}

![](kfold.png){width=80%}

:::
:::

## Example:

```{python}
#| echo: false
n_train, n_test = 400, 100
max_degree = 8
true_w = torch.zeros(max_degree)
true_w[0:5] = torch.tensor([-0.2, 0.4, 0.4, -1.194, 0.672], dtype=torch.float32)  # cubic truth
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
## Generate features
features = torch.randn(n_train + n_test, 1)   # N(0,1)
features = features * 2.4 + 3.5               # roughly mean 1.5, std 2  (covers ~[-3, 6])
features = torch.clamp(features, -8.0, 8)   # hard truncate to [-3, 6]

perm = torch.randperm(features.shape[0])
features = features[perm]
perm = torch.randperm(features.shape[0])
features = features[perm]

# Center x around the middle of the interval
x0 = 1.5                        # midpoint of [-3,6]
x_centered = features - x0

poly_features = torch.cat(
    [x_centered ** i / math.factorial(i) for i in range(max_degree)],
    dim=1
)

# generate labels from features and truth
labels = poly_features @ true_w
labels += 0.42 * torch.randn_like(labels)

## put them in your working sets
X_train, X_test = poly_features[:n_train], poly_features[n_train:]
y_train, y_test = labels[:n_train], labels[n_train:]
x_train_raw, x_test_raw = features[:n_train], features[n_train:]
```

```{python}
#| echo: false

## get the losses
def evaluate_loss(model, X, y):
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        pred = model(X)
        return nn.MSELoss()(pred, y).item()

## fit the model
def fit_polynomial(degree, num_epochs=19, lr=0.0001):
    model = nn.Linear(degree, 1, bias=False)
    loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()
    opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=lr)

    ds = TensorDataset(X_train[:, :degree], y_train)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=min(10, n_train), shuffle=True)

    train_losses, test_losses = [], []

    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        model.train()
        for xb, yb in dl:
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()

        train_losses.append(evaluate_loss(model, X_train[:, :degree], y_train))
        test_losses.append(evaluate_loss(model, X_test[:, :degree], y_test))

    return model, train_losses, test_losses
```

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
### We want to estimate a polynomial:

$$
{\large
    Y = \mathcal{P}_d(X) =  \sum_{i=0}^{d} x^i w_i
}%
$$

![](poly_scatter.png){width=90%}

:::
::: {.column width="44%"}
### Our Candidates are:

$$
    \hat{Y} = \mathcal{P}_2(X)    \\    
    \hat{Y} = \mathcal{P}_3(X)    \\    
    \hat{Y} = \mathcal{P}_4(X)    \\    
    \hat{Y} = \mathcal{P}_6(X)    
$$

![](candidates.png){width=80%}

:::
:::

## Our Fit gives us that

```{python}
#| echo: false
results = {}
for degree in [3, 4, 5, 7]:
    results[degree] = fit_polynomial(degree)
``` 

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="74%"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
x_grid = torch.linspace(features.min().item() - 0.2,
                        features.max().item() + 0.2, 400).reshape(-1, 1)
grid_poly = torch.cat(
    [x_grid ** i / math.factorial(i) for i in range(max_degree)],
    dim=1
)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(9, 6), sharey=True)
axes = axes.ravel()

# Fixed view window, same for all panels
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlim(-2.0, 9.0)   # x-range you care about
    ax.set_ylim(-2.5, 6)   # fixed y-range (e.g. just a bit beyond your y extremes)

for ax, degree in zip(axes, [3, 4, 5, 7]):
    model, train_losses, test_losses = results[degree]
    with torch.no_grad():
        y_hat = model(grid_poly[:, :degree]).numpy()

    ax.scatter(x_train_raw.numpy(), y_train.numpy(), s=2, alpha=0.8, label="train")
    ax.scatter(x_test_raw.numpy(), y_test.numpy(), s=2, alpha=0.8, label="test")
    ax.plot(x_grid.numpy(), y_hat, lw=2.5, label=f"degree {degree-1}")
    ax.set_title(f"{degree-1} degree polynomial")
    ax.set_xlabel("x")
    ax.grid(alpha=0.25)

axes[0].set_ylabel("y")
axes[2].set_ylabel("y")
axes[0].legend(frameon=False)
fig.suptitle("Polynomial fitting: underfit vs. good fit vs. overfit", y=0.98)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

::: {.column width="24%"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Collect all estimated weights
estimated_weights = {}
max_deg = max(results.keys())
max_k = max_deg  # max number of coefficients we ever see

for degree in sorted(results.keys()):
    model, train_losses, test_losses = results[degree]
    w_hat = model.weight.detach().cpu().reshape(-1).numpy()
    estimated_weights[degree] = np.round(w_hat,4)

# ensure consistent ordering of columns
degrees = [3, 4, 5, 7]

# build DataFrame, padding shorter columns with NaN
df = pd.DataFrame(
    {deg: pd.Series(estimated_weights[deg]) for deg in degrees}
)

# set row index as generic weight index (0..max_k-1)
df.index = np.arange(df.shape[0])
df.index.name = "w"

def fmt_trunc(v):
    # If v is NaN, show empty string or 'NaN'
    if isinstance(v, float) and math.isnan(v):
        return ""  # or "NaN"
    # truncate to 4 decimals
    v_trunc = int(v * 10_000) / 10_000.0
    s = f"{v_trunc:.2f}"
    s = s.rstrip("0").rstrip(".")
    return s

styled = (
    df.style
      .format(fmt_trunc)
)

styled
```

:::

:::

## Training Process

Plot the Loss:

```{python}
#| echo: false
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6), sharey=True)
axes = axes.ravel()

for ax, degree in zip(axes, [3, 4, 5, 7]):
    model, train_losses, test_losses = results[degree]
    ax.plot(train_losses, lw=2, label="train loss")
    ax.plot(test_losses, lw=2, label="test loss")
    ax.set_title(f"{degree-1} degree")
    ax.set_xlabel("epoch")
    ax.set_yscale("log")
    ax.grid(alpha=0.25)

axes[0].set_ylabel("MSE loss (log scale)")
axes[2].set_ylabel("MSE loss (log scale)")
axes[0].legend(frameon=False)

fig.suptitle("Generalization gap by model complexity", y=0.98)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Cross-Validation

We use K-Fold to Validate our model:

```{python}
#| echo: false
K = 5
n_samples = poly_features.shape[0]
indices = np.arange(n_samples)
np.random.seed(0)  # for reproducibility
np.random.shuffle(indices)

folds = np.array_split(indices, K)
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
def fit_polynomial_on_split(X_all, y_all, train_idx, val_idx, degree,
                            num_epochs=30, lr=0.0001):
    model = nn.Linear(degree, 1, bias=False)
    loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()
    opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=lr)

    X_train = X_all[train_idx, :degree]
    y_train = y_all[train_idx]
    X_val   = X_all[val_idx, :degree]
    y_val   = y_all[val_idx]

    ds = TensorDataset(X_train, y_train)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=min(10, len(train_idx)), shuffle=True)

    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        model.train()
        for xb, yb in dl:
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()

    # one validation MSE at the end
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_pred = model(X_val)
        val_loss = loss_fn(val_pred, y_val).item()

    return model, val_loss
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
degrees = [3, 4, 5, 7]  # or any list you like
cv_results = {deg: [] for deg in degrees}

for degree in degrees:
    for k in range(K):
        val_idx = folds[k]
        train_idx = np.concatenate([folds[j] for j in range(K) if j != k])

        model, val_loss = fit_polynomial_on_split(
            poly_features, labels, train_idx, val_idx, degree
        )
        cv_results[degree].append(val_loss)

print("5-fold cross-validation MSE per degree:\n")
```

:::{layout-ncol=2}
```{python}
#| echo: false
maxloss = np.max(np.mean(cv_results[degree]))
minloss = np.min(np.mean(cv_results[degree]))

top = maxloss + maxloss/100
floor = minloss - maxloss/100

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 3))
plt.bar([d-1 for d in degrees],
         [np.mean(cv_results[d]) for d in degrees])
plt.ylim(floor,top)
plt.xlabel("Polynomial degree (d-1)")
plt.ylabel("5-fold CV MSE")
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.title("MEAN SQUARED ERROR via K-fold cross-validation")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| echo: false

maxloss_std = np.max(np.std(cv_results[degree]))
minloss_std = np.min(np.std(cv_results[degree]))

top_std = maxloss_std + maxloss_std/8
floor_std = minloss_std - maxloss_std/40

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 3))
plt.bar([d-1 for d in degrees],
         [np.std(cv_results[d]) for d in degrees])
plt.ylim(floor_std,top_std)
plt.xlabel("Polynomial degree (d-1)")
plt.ylabel("5-fold CV MSE")
plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.title("Model selection via K-fold cross-validation")
plt.show()
```
:::


```{python}
#| echo: false
for degree in degrees:
    losses = cv_results[degree]
    mean_loss = np.mean(losses)
    std_loss = np.std(losses)
    print(f"degree {degree-1}: mean MSE={mean_loss:.4f}, std={std_loss:.4f}")
```

## Check Your Understanding {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.1rem; margin-top:1rem;"}

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**1. IID-Errors**

Give at least five examples where dependent random variables make treating the problem as IID data inadvisable.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b5282a; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**2. k-fold**

Why is the k-fold cross-validation very expensive to compute?

:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**3. Zero Errors**

Can you ever expect to see zero training error? Under which circumstances would you see zero generalization error?

:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b8860b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**4. Complexity**

The VC dimension is defined as the maximum number of points that can be classified with arbitrary labels 
by a function of a class of functions. Why might this not be a good idea for measuring how complex the class of functions is? Hint: consider the magnitude of the functions.

:::
:::

:::

## Part 5: Generalization, Regulations {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:2.2rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}
Weigt Decay
:::

::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:1rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
Rige Penalty in th Deep learning Context
:::

:::


## Weight Decay

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width=52%}
To avoid complexity we feed the Optimizer also a penalty for Magintude of weights:

$$
{\large
  min[L(w,b) + \color{"darkred"}{\frac{\lambda}{2} w^2}]
}%
$$

$$
{\large
w \leftarrow (1-\eta \lambda) w - \frac{\eta}{|\mathcal{B}|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} x{(i)} (w'w{(i)} + b - y^{(i)})
}%
$$
:::

:::{.column width=42%}

![](Ridge.png){width=100%}

:::
:::
## Example:
```{python}
#| echo: false
def fit_polynomial_on_split(X_all, y_all, train_idx, val_idx, degree,
                            num_epochs=30, lr=0.0002, wd=0.0):
    model = nn.Linear(degree, 1, bias=False)
    loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

    # This line implements the update rule from the chapter:
    # it minimizes L(w,b) + (lambda/2)||w||^2
    opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=lr, weight_decay=wd)

    X_train = X_all[train_idx, :degree]
    y_train = y_all[train_idx]
    X_val   = X_all[val_idx, :degree]
    y_val   = y_all[val_idx]

    ds = TensorDataset(X_train, y_train)
    dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=min(10, len(train_idx)), shuffle=True)

    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        model.train()
        for xb, yb in dl:
            opt.zero_grad()
            loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
            loss.backward()
            opt.step()

    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        val_pred = model(X_val)
        val_loss = loss_fn(val_pred, y_val).item()

    return model, val_loss
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
K = 5
# folds = np.array_split(indices, K)   # you already defined this earlier
degrees = [3, 4, 5, 7]
lambdas = [1e-3, 1e-2, 1e-1, 2e-1]  # e.g. no WD, mild WD, stronger WD

cv_results = {deg: {wd: [] for wd in lambdas} for deg in degrees}
best_models = {deg: {wd: None for wd in lambdas} for deg in degrees}


for degree in degrees:
    for wd in lambdas:
        best_loss = float("inf")
        for k in range(K):
            val_idx = folds[k]
            train_idx = np.concatenate([folds[j] for j in range(K) if j != k])

            model, val_loss = fit_polynomial_on_split(
                poly_features, labels,
                train_idx, val_idx,
                degree,
                num_epochs=30, lr=0.0001, wd=wd
            )
            cv_results[degree][wd].append(val_loss)

        if val_loss < best_loss:
            best_loss = val_loss
            best_models[degree][wd] = model

tables = {}  # degree -> DataFrame

for degree in degrees:
    rows = []
    for wd in lambdas:
        mean_loss = np.mean(cv_results[degree][wd])
        rows.append({"λ": wd, "MSE": mean_loss})
    df_deg = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    tables[degree] = df_deg
```

:::{layout="[[6,1]]"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
x_all = x_centered.cpu().numpy()  # original 1D inputs, shape (N,)
y_all = labels

x_plot = np.linspace(x_all.min(), x_all.max(), 200)
x_plot_centered = torch.tensor(x_plot, dtype=torch.float32).view(-1, 1)

X_plot_poly = torch.cat(
    [x_plot_centered ** i / math.factorial(i) for i in range(max_degree)],
    dim=1
)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(
    nrows=len(degrees),
    ncols=len(lambdas),
    figsize=(2.2*len(lambdas), 1.7*len(degrees)),
    
    sharex=True, sharey=True
)

for i, degree in enumerate(degrees):
    for j, wd in enumerate(lambdas):
        ax = axes[i, j]
        model = best_models[degree][wd]

        with torch.no_grad():
            y_hat = model(X_plot_poly[:, :degree]).numpy()

        ax.scatter(x_all, y_all.numpy(), s=1, alpha=0.8)
        ax.plot(x_plot, y_hat, color="red")

        ax.tick_params(axis="both", which="major", labelsize=8)

# column headers (lambdas) – put on top row
for j, wd in enumerate(lambdas):
    axes[0, j].set_title(rf"$\lambda={wd}$")

# row headers (degrees) – use ylabels or fig-side text
for i, degree in enumerate(degrees):
    # option A: use y-axis label
    axes[i, 0].set_ylabel(rf"$\deg={degree-1}$")

    # or option B: text in the left margin
    # fig.text(0.04, 
    #          axes[i, 0].get_position().centery,
    #          rf"deg={degree}", va="center", ha="right")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.ylim(-2, 8)
plt.xlim(-3.5, 7.5)
plt.show()
```

:::{.right}

K-Fold

```{python}
#| echo: false
tables[3].style.hide(axis="index")
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
tables[4].style.hide(axis="index").hide(axis="columns")
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
tables[4].style.hide(axis="index").hide(axis="columns")
```


```{python}
#| echo: false
tables[7].style.hide(axis="index").hide(axis="columns")
```
:::
:::

## Check Your Understanding {background-color="#fafaf8"}

::: {style="display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr; gap:1.1rem; margin-top:1rem;"}

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #1a3a6b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**1. Optimal Set?**

Use a validation set to find the optimal value of 
. Is it really the optimal value? Does this matter?
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b5282a; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**2. The `1/2` factor**

Review the relationship between training error and generalization error. In addition to weight decay, increased training, and the use of a model of suitable complexity, what other ways might help us deal with overfitting?
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #2d7a4f; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**3. Forgetting `zero_grad()`**

We know that 
. Can you find a similar equation for matrices
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {style="background:#f0f2f5; border-top:4px solid #b8860b; border-radius:8px; padding:1.1rem;"}
**4. One-line swap**

What would the update equations look like if instead of 
 we used 
 as our penalty of choice (
 regularization)?
:::
:::

:::

## Which one is it? ##

### Candidates

$$
{\large
    \hat{y} = \mathcal{P}_2(X)
}%    \\    
{\large
    \hat{y} = \mathcal{P}_3(X)
}%    \\    
{\large
    \hat{y} = \mathcal{P}_4(X)
}%    \\    
{\large
    \hat{y} = \mathcal{P}_6(X)    
}%
$$

### True Model

$$
{\Large
    y = 0.4 x + 0.2 x^2 - 0.199 x^3 + 0.028 x^4 - 1.5     \\     
}%
$$

### Though In Practice ###

Other models can serve a better Purpose than the truly specified one
$$
{\large
    \hat{y}_6 = \mathcal{P}_6(X) \succ \hat{y}_4 = \mathcal{P}_4(X)    \\    
}%
$$

:::{.columns}
:::{.column width=48%}

![](other6.png){width=90%}

:::

:::{.column width=48%}

![](tru4_2.png){width=90%}

:::
:::

## Thank You {background-color="#1a3a6b"}

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:72vh; gap:1rem;"}

::: {style="font-size:2.2rem; font-weight:800; color:#ffffff; text-align:center; font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;"}

:::

::: {style="color:#c5d4e8; font-size:1rem; text-align:center; font-style:italic;"}
Lets get Our Hands Dirty
:::

:::
